text.title: Testing Patterns (Cross-Language) Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every application of testing-patterns enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Every test follows Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) or Given-When-Then with blank lines separating the three sections.
rule.rationale: Visual separation makes intent grep-able and lets reviewers identify the failing phase from the diff alone.

rule.statement: Follow the Test Pyramid: ~70% unit, ~20% integration, ~10% E2E by count and CI runtime.
rule.rationale: Inverted pyramids (heavy E2E) produce 5-10x slower feedback loops and disproportionate flake rate per signal gained.

rule.statement: For ≥3 test cases that share most input fields, use the Builder or Object Mother pattern; for ≤2, inline literals are fine.
rule.rationale: Premature builders add indirection; ad-hoc copy-paste at 5+ cases creates a maintenance trap.

rule.statement: Each test owns its data lifecycle: create in arrange, destroy in teardown; never depend on data left by another test.
rule.rationale: Test-order dependence is the most common cause of 'works alone, breaks in suite' incidents.

rule.statement: UI tests use the Page Object Model: one class per screen, methods named after user intent (`login()`, not `clickSubmit()`).
rule.rationale: Intent-named methods survive UI restructure; selectors live in one place and break in one place.

rule.statement: Property-based tests are reserved for pure functions with a clear invariant; do not chain them through I/O.
rule.rationale: Property tests with side effects shrink poorly and produce confusing minimised counter-examples.
